section.title: A Refuge in Old Age
section.sectionRationale: An individual lament with no superscription, its language so closely echoes earlier Davidic prayers (compare Psalm 31, 35, 40, 70) that many read it as an old man's prayer, perhaps David near the end of his life, refusing to let fading strength silence his hope or his praise.
section.thesis: Though old age, mocking enemies, and failing strength threaten to silence hope, the aging believer clings to the God who has carried him from the womb, and resolves to keep proclaiming God's righteousness to the next generation until every ounce of strength is spent.
section.frameNote: Five movements: a cry for refuge, verses 1 to 4; a lifetime of praise recalled, verses 5 to 8; a plea not to be forsaken in old age, verses 9 to 13; undying hope that keeps proclaiming God to the next generation, verses 14 to 18; praise for God's restoring righteousness, verses 19 to 24.

verses.text: In You, O LORD, I have taken refuge; let me never be put to shame.
In Your justice, rescue and deliver me; incline Your ear and save me.
Be my {{tsur|rock}} of refuge, where I can always go. Give the command to save me, for You are my rock and my fortress.
Deliver me, O my God, from the hand of the wicked, from the grasp of the unjust and ruthless.
For You are my hope, O Lord GOD, my confidence from my youth.
I have leaned on You since birth; You pulled me from my mother's womb. My praise is always for You.
I have become a portent to many, but You are my strong refuge.
My mouth is filled with Your praise and with Your splendor all day long.

units.label: Do not forsake me in old age

verses.text: Do not discard me in my old age; do not forsake me when my strength fails.
For my enemies speak against me, and those who lie in wait for my life conspire,
saying, "God has forsaken him; pursue him and seize him, for there is no one to rescue him."
Be not far from me, O God. Hurry, O my God, to help me.
May the accusers of my soul be ashamed and consumed; may those who seek my harm be covered with scorn and disgrace.

units.label: Undying hope
units.note: proclaiming God to the next generation

verses.text: But I will always {{tiqvah|hope}} and will praise You more and more.
My mouth will declare Your righteousness and Your salvation all day long, though I cannot know their full measure.
I will come in the strength of the Lord GOD; I will proclaim Your righteousness, Yours alone.
O God, You have taught me from my youth, and to this day I proclaim Your marvelous deeds.
Even when I am old and gray, do not forsake me, O God, until I proclaim Your power to the next generation, Your might to all who are to come.
Your righteousness reaches to the heavens, O God, You who have done great things. Who, O God, is like You?
Though You have shown me many troubles and misfortunes, You will revive me once again. Even from the depths of the earth You will bring me back up.
You will increase my honor and comfort me once again.
So I will praise You with the harp for Your faithfulness, O my God; I will sing praise to You with the lyre, O Holy One of Israel.
When I sing praise to You my lips will shout for joy, along with my soul, which You have redeemed.
My tongue will indeed proclaim Your righteousness all day long, for those who seek my harm are disgraced and confounded.

tsur.gloss: Rock, cliff, stronghold. Paired here with fortress, it pictures God as unmoving refuge, a place the psalmist can always return to no matter how old or weak he grows.

tiqvah.gloss: Hope, expectation, a cord stretched forward toward what is coming. The psalmist's hope is not wishful thinking but a forward-leaning trust that keeps producing praise, more and more, as life goes on.

basic.context: This psalm has no superscription, but its language echoes earlier prayers of David so closely that many read it as his prayer in old age, perhaps during Absalom's revolt, when the king was aged, weakened, and mocked by enemies who said God had abandoned him.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the honest prayer of a believer whose body is failing and whose enemies taunt him, yet who refuses to let weakness become an excuse for silence. It models, rather than commands, how faith perseveres into old age.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalmist calls God his tsur, rock, an unmoving refuge, and clings to tiqvah, hope, a forward-leaning expectation rather than idle wishing. Both words anchor a life that keeps praising even as strength fails.
basic.typology: The plea to be sustained from the womb into old age and gray hairs points to Christ, who never abandons His own from infancy to the grave, and the resolve to proclaim God's might to the next generation anticipates the church's ongoing witness to Christ across every generation.
basic.christ: The enemies' taunt in verse 11, 'God has forsaken him; there is no one to rescue him,' is echoed almost word for word at the cross, 'He trusts in God. Let God rescue Him now if He wants Him,' Matthew 27:43. The psalmist's confidence that God did not, in the end, abandon him anticipates the greater truth that God did not abandon His Son to the grave.
basic.god: God is the psalmist's refuge from birth to old age, a rock who does not withdraw when strength fails, and who revives His people even from the depths of the earth.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 27:43 echoes the very taunt of verse 11 against Jesus at the cross. Isaiah 46:3-4 promises God will carry His people to old age and gray hairs. 2 Timothy 4:16-18 shows Paul, near the end of his life, rescued and still proclaiming.
basic.oneStory: This lament of an aging believer's undying hope threads into the whole Bible's story of God's unbroken faithfulness from birth to death, fulfilled in Christ, who was mocked with these very words yet was not abandoned by the Father, guaranteeing that all who take refuge in Him will be carried through to glory.

context.literary: An individual lament without a superscription, moving from a cry for refuge, through testimony of lifelong dependence, to a specific plea about old age, and finally to confident praise, with vocabulary and phrases closely paralleling Psalms 22, 31, and 35.
context.historical: Many place this prayer late in David's life, perhaps during Absalom's revolt, when the aging king faced betrayal and the taunt that God had forsaken him, 2 Samuel 15 to 18.
context.canonical: Positioned near the end of Book II, this psalm gives voice to every believer who fears that weakness or age will end God's care, and its taunt in verse 11 becomes, centuries later, the very words hurled at Jesus on the cross.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To model honest lament and undying hope for a believer whose body is failing and whose enemies mock his faith, resolving to keep proclaiming God's righteousness for as long as breath remains.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes one aging believer's specific fears and hopes; it prescribes, as an abiding pattern, taking refuge in God through every season of weakness and resolving to keep declaring His works to the next generation.

debates.question: Is this psalm David's own prayer in old age, or a later composition written for use by any aging worshiper?

views.label: A genuine prayer of David in his later years
views.case: The vocabulary and situation closely match David's circumstances during Absalom's revolt, and its placement near Psalm 70, a Davidic psalm, supports common authorship.

views.label: An anonymous liturgical prayer composed for the aged in Israel generally
views.case: The lack of any superscription, unusual in Book II, may indicate the psalm was deliberately left open for any aging worshiper to pray as his own.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm functions as Scripture's provided prayer for facing old age and weakening strength without losing hope in God.

study.originalLanguages: God is called tsur, rock, verse 3, an unmoving refuge paired with fortress imagery. The psalmist's tiqvah, hope, verse 14, is not passive wishing but an active, forward-leaning trust that keeps generating praise even as the body weakens.

typology.type: The taunt 'God has forsaken him,' verse 11
typology.fulfillment: Echoed almost word for word against Jesus at the cross, Matthew 27:43, where the true Sufferer bore the ultimate version of this mockery.

typology.type: Sustained from the womb to old age, verses 6, 9, 18
typology.fulfillment: Points to Christ's unbroken care for His people across every stage of life, and to the church's calling to proclaim His works to every coming generation.

study.christ: Verse 11's taunt, 'God has forsaken him; there is no one to rescue him,' is picked up almost verbatim by the crowd at the cross, 'He trusts in God. Let God rescue Him now if He wants Him,' Matthew 27:43. The psalmist's vindication, that God did not abandon him despite the taunt, anticipates the greater vindication of Christ, who was not, in the end, abandoned by the Father but raised in power.
study.god: God is portrayed as a lifelong refuge, sustaining the psalmist from birth, refusing to discard him in old age, and reviving him even from the depths of the earth, all while his righteousness reaches to the heavens.

crossReferences.note: The crowd's taunt at the cross echoes almost word for word the enemies' taunt in verse 11.
God promises to carry His people even to old age and gray hairs, matching this psalm's central plea.
Paul, near the end of his life, is rescued from the lion's mouth and continues to proclaim, paralleling the psalmist's resolve.

study.oneStory: This aging believer's refusal to let weakness silence hope threads into the Bible's larger story of God's unbroken faithfulness from birth to death, fulfilled in Christ, who bore this psalm's very taunt at the cross yet was vindicated, securing every believer's hope of being carried through every season to glory.

sermon.title: Seven Resolutions for Finishing Well
sermon.why: Preached from Psalm 71 at a funeral, Piper draws seven resolutions for aging and dying well straight from this psalm's own language of refuge, remembered grace, undying hope, and praise that refuses to grow quiet.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist ask God not to do as he grows old?

options.text: Discard him in his old age or forsake him when his strength fails, verse 9.
options.note: This is the psalm's central plea, repeated again in verse 18.

options.text: Give him more wealth in his later years.
options.note: The psalm is not concerned with wealth but with God's continued presence.

options.text: Remove all his enemies immediately.
options.note: He does ask for rescue from enemies, but the central plea concerns being forsaken in old age.

options.text: Let him die quickly and painlessly.
options.note: The psalm never asks for a quick death; it asks to keep proclaiming God as long as life lasts.

quiz.q: What do the psalmist's enemies say about him, according to verse 11?

options.text: "God has forsaken him; pursue him and seize him, for there is no one to rescue him."
options.note: This taunt is later echoed almost word for word against Jesus at the cross.

options.text: "He has always been faithful; leave him in peace."
options.note: This is the opposite of the enemies' mocking taunt.

options.text: "He is too powerful to be defeated."
options.note: The enemies mock his apparent weakness and abandonment, not his power.

options.text: "His God has never existed."
options.note: Close in spirit but not the actual wording; they claim God has abandoned him, not that God does not exist.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist resolve to do even in old age and gray hairs, verse 18?

options.text: Proclaim God's power and might to the next generation and to all who are to come.
options.note: This is his explicit resolve, the reason he asks not to be forsaken yet.

options.text: Retire from all worship and public life.
options.note: The opposite; he insists on continuing to proclaim God's works.

options.text: Seek comfort only in his family's wealth.
options.note: His comfort is in declaring God's works, not accumulated wealth.

options.text: Wait silently for death without speaking further.
options.note: He explicitly resolves to keep speaking, not to fall silent.

quiz.q: What confidence does the psalmist express about restoration in verses 20-21?

options.text: That God will revive him again and bring him back up even from the depths of the earth.
options.note: Despite many troubles, he trusts God to restore and honor him again.

options.text: That he will never face any more trouble again.
options.note: He acknowledges troubles have come and may come; his hope is in God's revival, not the absence of trouble.

options.text: That his enemies will become his friends.
options.note: The psalm expects the enemies to be disgraced, not befriended.

options.text: That his physical strength will never fail.
options.note: He already acknowledges his strength is failing; his hope is in God's power, not unfailing physical strength.

quiz.q: How does this psalm connect most directly to the New Testament?

options.text: The taunt of verse 11 is echoed almost word for word against Jesus at the cross, Matthew 27:43.
options.note: This is the psalm's most striking and direct New Testament connection.

options.text: It predicts the exact number of years Jesus would live.
options.note: The psalm makes no such prediction.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to the New Testament.
options.note: The connection to the cross's taunt is a direct and striking one.

options.text: It describes a miracle Jesus later performed.
options.note: The psalm is a prayer, not an account of a miracle, though its language of revival anticipates resurrection hope.

questions: The psalmist has leaned on God since birth, verse 6. Where can you trace God's faithfulness across your own life, from childhood until now?
He fears being forsaken specifically in old age and weakness, verse 9. What losses of strength, health, or usefulness make you most afraid that God might let you go?
The enemies' taunt in verse 11 is echoed against Jesus Himself at the cross. How does knowing that Christ bore this very mockery change how you face your own seasons of feeling forsaken?
The psalmist resolves to keep proclaiming God's works to the next generation even in old age, verse 18. Who is in your life that needs to hear what God has done for you?
Despite many troubles, verse 20, he trusts God to revive him again. Where do you need that same confidence today, that God can still bring you back up from a low place?

translationNotes.note: Rock and fortress translate two related Hebrew military terms for a stronghold; paired together they intensify the picture of an unmoving refuge the psalmist can always run to.

The Hebrew verb behind 'pulled me' is somewhat unusual, and some render it 'you are my support' from birth rather than 'you pulled me forth'; either way the verse affirms God's care from the very beginning of life.

The title 'Lord GOD' renders the divine name paired with a title of sovereignty, used twice in this psalm, verses 5 and 16, underscoring that the psalmist's hope rests in God's supreme authority, not his own strength.

No em or en dashes present after editing verse 16. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming John Piper's 'Seven Resolutions for Finishing Well' at desiringgod.org, preached from Psalm 71.
